description:
Got a weird one — our internal health-checker at /home/user/netcheck/checker.py is supposed to hit three endpoints and dump a json summary to /home/user/netcheck/results.json. It's been running fine for months but now it just hangs forever when I run it. Doesn't error, doesn't timeout, just sits there.

The endpoints are all mocked locally (little flask apps) so there's no external dependency. Pretty sure they're up — I can curl them manually. configs are in /home/user/netcheck/endpoints.yaml. Maybe something got misconfigured when someone reorganized the project last week? I don't really have time to dig through it, just need the checker working again so it writes that results.json with status for all three services.
truth:
Initial state:
- /home/user/netcheck/checker.py is a Python script that reads endpoints.yaml, makes HTTP requests to each endpoint, and writes results.json with the status of each.
- /home/user/netcheck/endpoints.yaml contains three endpoint configs:
    ```yaml
    endpoints:
      - name: auth-service
        url: http://127.0.0.1:5001/health
        timeout: 5
      - name: data-service
        url: http://127.0.0.1:5002/health
        timeout: 5
      - name: cache-service
        url: http://127.0.0.1:5003/health
        timeout: 5
    ```
- Three Flask apps are running as background processes:
    - auth-service on port 5001 (healthy, responds to /health)
    - data-service on port 5002 (healthy, responds to /health)
    - cache-service on port 5003 (healthy, responds to /health)
- The bug: checker.py has a subtle issue introduced during "reorganization" — it reads the timeout value from the config but passes it to requests.get() incorrectly. The code does:
    ```python
    timeout_val = endpoint.get('timeout', 5)
    # ... later in the code ...
    resp = requests.get(url, timeout=config.get('global_timeout'))
    ```
    The code references a nonexistent 'global_timeout' key in the top-level config dict, which returns None. requests.get(timeout=None) means "wait forever."
- There's also a /home/user/netcheck/config.py that was added during reorganization with a DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 10 constant that's imported but never used in checker.py (red herring / evidence of incomplete refactor).
- curl to all three endpoints works: `curl http://127.0.0.1:5001/health` etc. all return {"status": "ok"}.
- Python 3.11, requests library installed. /home/user/netcheck is writable.
- results.json does not currently exist (checker hangs before writing it).

Expected final state:
- `cd /home/user/netcheck && timeout 30 python checker.py` exits 0 within 30 seconds.
- /home/user/netcheck/results.json exists and is valid JSON.
- results.json contains status entries for all three services (auth-service, data-service, cache-service), each showing a successful/healthy status.
- The fix correctly uses the per-endpoint timeout values from endpoints.yaml (or reasonable hardcoded defaults), not None.

Invariants:
- endpoints.yaml endpoint URLs unchanged (still pointing to 5001, 5002, 5003).
- All three Flask services still running after the task.
- The checker still reads from endpoints.yaml (not hardcoded endpoint list).

Anti-shortcut guards:
- checker.py must actually make HTTP requests — `grep -E 'requests\.get|urllib|http\.client' /home/user/netcheck/checker.py` must match.
- results.json must not be manually created without running checker.py — the file's mtime must be after task start, and checker.py must have been executed.
- The fix must address the timeout issue, not just wrap the whole script in a subprocess with external timeout.
name: task_000000_7ebb507d
